source: ISSN Position Stand: Protein and Exercise (JISSN 2017) — doi:10.1186/s12970-017-0177-8
note: Values are g of protein per kg of body weight per day. Not medical advice.

muscle_gain_hypertrophy.practical_note: Upper end (2.0–2.2 g/kg) beneficial when in caloric deficit to preserve muscle.

fat_loss_body_recomposition.practical_note: Higher protein preserves lean mass during caloric restriction. Vegetarians need careful planning.

endurance_performance.practical_note: Focus on carbs; protein supports muscle repair on heavy training days.

general_fitness_maintenance.practical_note: Most exercising individuals fall here comfortably.

sedentary_general_health.practical_note: Standard RDA. Increase toward 1.2 g/kg if doing any resistance training.

vegetarian_plant_based_notes.leucine_gap: Plant proteins have lower leucine density than animal proteins. Compensate by eating slightly more total protein (add 10–20%) or prioritising high-leucine plants: soy, edamame, chickpeas, lentils.
complete_protein_combos: Quinoa (already complete — all 9 essential amino acids)
vegetarian_plant_based_notes.digestibility_note: Plant protein DIAAS (digestibility score) is lower than whey/egg. ISSN recommends vegetarian athletes eat toward upper end of protein range.

high_protein_vegetarian_foods_per_100g.note: Approximate values — verify with USDA FoodData Central for precision.

foods.name: Chickpeas, cooked
foods.notes: Pair with rice for complete profile

foods.name: Almonds
foods.notes: Good but fat-dense; eat in moderation
